ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Royyala Iguru or Andhra prawn curry. FOODON_03302051 (shrimp dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for Andhra-style prawn curry specifically. A0CKP (Crustaceans, cooked) used as closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for Royyala Iguru or Andhra prawn curry as a specific clinical food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Royyala Iguru is approximately 250 g, comprising a generous portion of prawns in a thick spiced onion-tomato masala gravy, usually served alongside steamed rice or roti.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Prawn (cooked, spiced preparation) and component ingredients
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for Royyala Iguru as a composite dish. Values are derived from ICMR-NIN entries for cooked prawns/shrimp and standard Andhra masala components (onion, tomato, oil, spices). Prawn component dominates protein and fat; masala base contributes carbohydrate, sodium, and fibre. Weighted composite estimate based on typical recipe proportions: ~60% prawns, ~20% onion-tomato base, ~10% oil, ~10% spices and aromatics.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Seafood and protein-dominant mixed dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Royyala Iguru. GI estimated at 25 based on analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood curry dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. Prawns and protein-dominant dishes with minimal starchy carbohydrate consistently yield GI values in the 20–30 range. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: (25 × 5.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 3.25, rounded to 3.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g) from spice blends, salt, and tomato base
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g or less per serving and request reduced-salt preparation, as a 250 g restaurant serving delivers approximately 1050 mg sodium, representing nearly half the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~25) and low net carbohydrate content (~5.2 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Royyala Iguru is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions, as its high protein content (~14.8 g per 100 g), low GI (~25), and low glycemic load (~3.3 per serving) support stable postprandial blood glucose; pair with low-GI rice or roti rather than white rice to further minimise glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content (~148 mg per 100 g) from prawns and moderate total fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Prawns are naturally high in dietary cholesterol (~148 mg per 100 g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit servings to 150 g and ensure the dish is prepared with unsaturated cooking oil rather than ghee or palm oil, as the saturated fat content is relatively low (~1.4 g per 100 g) when prepared with vegetable oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Royyala Iguru (Telugu: రొయ్యల ఇగురు) is a traditional Andhra Pradesh prawn curry from South India, characterised by its bold, fiery spice profile and thick, dry-ish masala coating. The dish is prepared by sautéing fresh prawns with a base of finely chopped onions and tomatoes, then tossing them with a blend of ground spices including red chilli, turmeric, coriander, cumin, garam masala, ginger, garlic, and fresh curry leaves. Unlike North Indian prawn curries that use cream or yoghurt, Royyala Iguru is oil-based and relies on the natural sweetness of caramelised onions and the acidity of tomatoes to balance the heat. The texture is semi-dry with a thick, clingy masala that coats each prawn. The flavour is intensely spiced, slightly tangy, and aromatic. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish with a low glycemic index (~25), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The sodium content is moderate to high (~420 mg per 100 g) due to added salt and spice blends, which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension. Prawns provide lean protein (~14.8 g per 100 g), vitamin B12, selenium, and phosphorus. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. It is commonly served with steamed rice, biryani rice, or Indian flatbreads such as roti or parotta. In Singapore, it is served at Andhra and Telugu cuisine restaurants.
common_user_queries: Is Royyala Iguru good for diabetics?
What spices are in Royyala Iguru?

confidence_reason: No single ICMR-NIN or approved-source entry exists for Royyala Iguru as a composite dish. Nutrient values are derived from ICMR-NIN data for cooked prawns and standard Andhra masala components using weighted recipe proportions. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on comparable high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood curry dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the primary ingredient (prawns) is well-characterised in ICMR-NIN and the recipe composition is well-documented in culinary literature.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for prawn preparations. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero) as this dish is prepared with vegetable oil and contains no hydrogenated fats. Nutrient values represent a weighted composite estimate based on typical Andhra-style recipe proportions (~60% prawns, ~20% onion-tomato base, ~10% oil, ~10% spices and aromatics) derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Royyala Iguru was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. Halal status is assumed based on typical preparation without pork or alcohol; kosher status is not confirmed. The dish is served at Manam - Upper Circular Road restaurant on field survey data at SGD 23.00.
